reasoning: The primary probability anchor from the FRED statistical trend analysis yields an estimate of P(increase above 4.12) = 0.30 within the next period, largely because a purely linear autoregressive model predicts strong mean-reversion from such a steep spike (projecting a baseline closer to 3.41). However, the web search reveals an exogenous geopolitical shock not captured by the historical model: an ongoing war with Iran heavily impacting the Strait of Hormuz. Because wholesale gasoline momentum remains strong and typically leads retail pump prices by 1-2 weeks, the short-term probability has been updated significantly upward against the anchor. I predict strong chances of further increases in the short term (from 1 week to 3 months) as the war panic and summer driving season collide. By 6 months (October 2026), the transition to winter blend gasoline and general market adaptation strongly suggest mean-reversion, pulling the forecast to <0.5. Standard multi-year mean-reversion following an extreme price spike dominates the mid-range targets (1 to 3 years), keeping P < 0.5. As the forecast horizon extends to 5 and 10 years, massive long-term uncertainties (currency inflation vs. widespread EV adoption driving demand destruction) force the probabilities strictly closer to 0.5 in accordance with instructions.
